title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models

classification.reasoning: The paper empirically derives power‑law relationships between loss, model size, data amount, and compute, and uses them to recommend compute‑optimal training strategies that favor larger models trained on relatively modest data. Its primary aim is to guide how to obtain more capable language models through scaling, not to reduce the cost of running existing models. The only “efficiency” aspect is the compute‑optimal allocation, which serves the goal of better capability, not cheaper inference.
classification.justification: The work contributes scaling laws and a framework for compute‑optimal training, showing how loss improves predictably with increased parameters, data, and compute. This directly addresses the professor’s question about making LLMs more capable via scale. It does not propose techniques like quantization, pruning, or other inference‑time reductions, so it does not belong to the efficiency category. The paper’s discussion of sample‑efficiency and early stopping is framed as a way to achieve higher performance for a given compute budget, reinforcing the scaling focus.

The only efficiency‑related angle is that the resulting model is cheaper to run, but that is a by‑product, not the research goal.

title: FlashAttention :   Fast   and   Memory-Eﬃcient   Exact   Attention with   IO-Awareness

classification.reasoning: The paper introduces FlashAttention, an exact attention kernel that reduces memory reads/writes and speeds up self‑attention, directly targeting faster, cheaper training and inference of existing transformer sizes. Its primary goal is to improve runtime and memory efficiency, not to enlarge models or study scaling laws; the capability gains (longer context, better perplexity) are secondary benefits of the efficiency improvement. The only plausible scaling argument is that the method enables longer contexts, but the contribution itself is an efficiency technique, not a scaling strategy.
classification.justification: (1) The paper contributes a new IO‑aware attention algorithm and a block‑sparse variant that lower HBM traffic and wall‑clock time. (2) This is an efficiency contribution aimed at reducing compute and memory cost for fixed‑size models, not at training larger or more capable models. (3) Hence it directly answers the professor’s efficiency question about making LLMs run faster and with less memory.

title: Emergent   Abilities   of   Large   Language   Models

classification.reasoning: The paper investigates how new capabilities appear only when language models are scaled up, defining and cataloguing emergent abilities, and questioning whether further scaling will yield more. Its primary focus is on understanding and extending model capability via scale, not on reducing compute, memory, or inference cost. The only possible counter‑argument is that it surveys literature without proposing new methods, but this does not shift the focus toward efficiency; the work remains centered on capability growth.

title: SparseGPT: Massive Language Models Can be Accurately Pruned in One-Shot

classification.reasoning: The paper addresses the high computational and memory cost of running very large GPT models by introducing a one‑shot pruning technique that creates sparse versions with 50‑60% unstructured sparsity, directly reducing inference FLOPs and memory without retraining. Its primary aim is to make existing massive models cheaper to deploy, not to increase their capability or train larger models. The only scaling‑related aspect is that larger models prune more easily, but this is a side observation rather than a goal of achieving more capable models.
classification.justification: SparseGPT contributes a post‑training pruning method that yields highly sparse, inference‑efficient versions of OPT‑175B and BLOOM‑176B, enabling faster, lower‑memory inference. This aligns with the efficiency objective of reducing the cost of running LLMs at their current scale. It does not propose new training regimes, data scaling, or capability improvements, so it does not fit the scaling category. The work directly answers the professor’s question about making LLMs more efficient.

title: QLORA:  Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs

classification.reasoning: The paper tackles the prohibitive memory and compute cost of fine‑tuning very large LLMs by introducing QLoRA, which combines 4‑bit quantization (NF4) with LoRA adapters and memory‑saving tricks to enable full‑performance fine‑tuning of 33‑B and 65‑B models on a single GPU. The primary aim is to make existing large models cheaper and more accessible, not to train larger or more capable models, so the efficiency framing dominates. The only plausible scaling argument is that larger models become feasible to fine‑tune, but this is a consequence of the efficiency technique, not the paper’s central goal.
classification.justification: The contribution is a set of quantization and PEFT methods (NF4, double quantization, paged optimizers) that dramatically cut memory/compute for fine‑tuning while matching 16‑bit performance. This directly addresses the problem of costly fine‑tuning of existing model sizes, placing it firmly in the efficiency domain. It does not propose new scaling laws, larger model training regimes, or capability‑focused studies, so it does not belong to the scaling category. This aligns with the professor’s question of whether the work makes LLMs more efficient.
